content.text: What It Is
Toil identification and reduction programs

are systematic initiatives that locate, measure, prioritize, and eliminate manual, repetitive, automatable work—called toil—from the daily operations of engineering teams. Borrowed from Google SRE practice and extended for agentic AI systems, these programs treat the reduction of toil as a first-class engineering investment, not an afterthought.

Google's Site Reliability Engineering book defines toil as work that is: manual, repetitive, automatable, tactical (reactive rather than strategic), has no enduring value, and scales linearly with service growth. Every bullet in that definition is load-bearing. Work that is repetitive but not automatable is not toil—it is fundamental human judgment. Work that is manual but endures (such as a postmortem write-up) is not toil—it is knowledge creation. The precision matters because misclassifying work as toil leads to automating things that should not be automated.

Agentic AI operations introduce categories of toil that have no equivalent in traditional software: manually adjusting prompts when model behavior drifts, managing context window overflow for long-running agents, executing model version rollout runbooks step by step, and triaging agent failures caused by non-deterministic model outputs. A mature toil program explicitly names and tracks these AI-specific categories alongside conventional SRE toil.
Why It Matters

Google's SRE teams enforce a rule: toil should consume no more than 50% of any SRE's working time. When toil exceeds 50%, engineers stop improving the system and begin merely maintaining it. Over months, toil expands to fill all available capacity, new features become riskier to deploy because the team is stretched thin, and engineers leave because their work feels like running on a treadmill.

For agentic AI services, the toil problem is amplified by the non-deterministic nature of the AI layer. Conventional services fail predictably: a database goes down, a network partition occurs, a memory leak manifests. Agentic services fail unpredictably: the model starts choosing the wrong tool, context accumulates incorrectly across sessions, or a model version update shifts output format subtly enough to break downstream parsing but not enough to trigger obvious alerts. Each of these failures may require manual investigation and intervention that resists automation precisely because the failure mode is novel each time.
Toil compounds:

a service that requires four hours of manual work per week at current scale will require eight hours at 2x scale if the work is not automated. Toil grows with the service unless explicitly eliminated.
Toil masks capacity:
engineers buried in toil cannot work on reliability improvements, feature development, or technical debt reduction. The team appears busy while the product stagnates.
Toil is a retention risk:
experienced engineers who spend the majority of their time on manual operational work leave for roles where they can build and improve. Institutional knowledge walks out with them.
LLM toil is often invisible:
prompt tweaking, context management, and model-version validation are done ad hoc, not tracked in any system, and therefore invisible to leadership and capacity planning.
Benefits
Released engineering capacity:
every hour of toil eliminated is an hour available for reliability improvement, feature development, or technical debt reduction.
Reduced human error:
manual processes executed under time pressure produce mistakes. Automated equivalents are consistent and auditable.
Scalability:
automation scales with traffic growth at near-zero marginal cost. Manual toil scales linearly, requiring proportional headcount.
Improved on-call experience:
reducing interrupt-driven toil makes on-call shifts sustainable, improving rotation coverage and reducing burnout.
Organizational visibility:
quantifying toil in hours and dollars gives leadership a clear ROI frame for investing in automation infrastructure.
Agent-mediated toil reduction:
agentic AI systems can themselves automate categories of operational toil—a unique advantage of AI-native operations teams.
Trade-offs
Automation cost vs. toil cost:
automating a task that occurs twice a month is often not worth the engineering investment. Prioritization frameworks must account for the amortized cost of building the automation.
Automation brittleness:

automated processes that depend on brittle integrations can produce more toil (maintenance of the automation itself) than the original manual process. Toil reduction programs must track automation maintenance cost as a new toil category.
LLM automation risk:

using LLM agents to automate operational tasks introduces new failure modes. An agent automating runbook execution can take incorrect actions if the runbook is ambiguous or the situation is novel. Human-in-the-loop escalation paths are required.
Measurement overhead:
systematic toil tracking requires engineers to log time against categories. This is itself small toil. Keep the logging mechanism lightweight — time-category surveys, not per-minute timesheets.
How It Works
The Google SRE Toil Definition Applied to AI Ops
Applying Google's six toil criteria to common agentic AI operations tasks helps identify what qualifies as toil and what does not:
Manual:
an engineer performs it by hand. Example: copying prompt versions into a test harness manually.
Repetitive:
done more than once and expected to recur. Example: running a model validation checklist before every model version deployment.
Automatable:
a machine could perform it with sufficient determinism. Example: checking that all tool-call schemas are valid after a schema library update.
Tactical:
reactive work driven by external events rather than engineering judgment. Example: restarting an agent process after a context overflow crash.
No enduring value:
doing it once produces nothing that persists beyond the immediate fix. Example: manually truncating a log file to free disk space.
O(n) with service growth:
work volume increases proportionally with traffic, users, or models deployed. Example: manually validating prompt behavior against a fixed set of examples for each new tenant.
Categories of Agent-Specific Toil
Agentic AI operations produce four distinct toil categories not present in conventional SRE work:
Prompt tweaking:

manual adjustment of system prompts or few-shot examples in response to model behavior drift, user complaints, or model version updates. This is toil when the trigger is predictable and the evaluation process is not automated. It is engineering when the adjustment requires novel judgment.
Context window management:

manually monitoring and truncating conversation histories or retrieved context to prevent context overflow errors. This is pure toil — a system that overflows its context window predictably is a system with a missing automated summarization or truncation step.
Model version rollouts:

executing multi-step runbooks to validate, stage, and promote a new model version. Manual rollout steps that follow the same sequence every time are automatable. The exception is the judgment call about whether evaluation results are acceptable — that remains human.
Runbook execution:

following step-by-step operational runbooks to respond to known failure modes. If a runbook has been executed three or more times in identical conditions, it is a candidate for automation or agent-mediated execution.
Architecture

The toil reduction lifecycle moves through four phases: identifying toil sources, quantifying their cost, prioritizing by impact and automation feasibility, and eliminating through automation or process redesign. The feedback loop measures the reduction achieved and feeds the next identification cycle.

attrs.alt: Toil identification and reduction lifecycle: Identify sources then Quantify cost then Prioritize by impact and effort then Eliminate via automation, with measurement feedback loop

content.text: When to Use It
When on-call burden is growing:
if engineers are spending more than two hours per on-call shift on manual responses to recurring issues, a toil reduction program is overdue.
When toil exceeds 33% of engineering time:
the Google SRE target is 50%, but addressing the problem before reaching 50% avoids the damage that occurs at that threshold.
When model version updates require extensive manual validation:
if upgrading to a new model version takes two days of manual testing, you will defer upgrades and accumulate technical debt.
When the same runbook has been executed five or more times:
this is the signal that the runbook is ripe for automation or agent-mediated execution.
When scaling is blocked by operational overhead:
if doubling traffic would require doubling the operations team, the work that would scale linearly with headcount is toil.
How to Implement
Step 1: Toil Survey and Inventory

Begin with a team survey. Ask each engineer to log their previous two weeks of work, tagging activities as toil or non-toil and labeling the toil category. Combine this with on-call interrupt logs and ticket triage time records to build a complete inventory.

# Toil survey data model and aggregation
# Ingest from Google Form / Jira time logs / PagerDuty alert history

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from collections import defaultdict
from typing import Literal

ToilCategory = Literal[
    "prompt_tweaking",
    "context_window_management",
    "model_version_rollout",
    "runbook_execution",
    "on_call_interrupt",
    "ticket_triage",
    "data_cleanup",
    "manual_deployment",
    "other"
]

@dataclass
class ToilEntry:
    engineer: str
    date: str           # ISO 8601
    category: ToilCategory
    hours: float
    description: str
    automatable: bool   # engineer's assessment
    recurrence: str     # "daily", "weekly", "monthly", "one-off"


def aggregate_toil(entries: list[ToilEntry]) -> dict:
    """Summarize toil hours by category and compute total eng time percentage."""
    category_hours: dict[str, float] = defaultdict(float)
    automatable_hours: float = 0.0
    total_hours: float = 0.0

    for e in entries:
        category_hours[e.category] += e.hours
        total_hours += e.hours
        if e.automatable:
            automatable_hours += e.hours

    # Assume 160 engineering hours/month per engineer (full time)
    num_engineers = len({e.engineer for e in entries})
    available_hours = num_engineers * 160
    toil_pct = (total_hours / available_hours) * 100 if available_hours > 0 else 0

    return {
        "total_toil_hours": total_hours,
        "toil_pct_of_capacity": round(toil_pct, 1),
        "automatable_hours": automatable_hours,
        "automatable_pct": round(automatable_hours / total_hours * 100, 1) if total_hours > 0 else 0,
        "by_category": dict(sorted(category_hours.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1])),
        "engineers": num_engineers,
    }

# Example output:
# {
#   "total_toil_hours": 148.5,
#   "toil_pct_of_capacity": 46.4,   <-- approaching the 50% danger zone
#   "automatable_hours": 97.0,
#   "automatable_pct": 65.3,
#   "by_category": {
#     "prompt_tweaking": 42.0,
#     "runbook_execution": 38.5,
#     "context_window_management": 29.0,
#     "on_call_interrupt": 21.0,
#     "ticket_triage": 18.0,
#   },
#   "engineers": 2
# }
Step 2: Toil Prioritization Scoring

Score each toil category on three dimensions: monthly hours saved if eliminated, automation feasibility (1-5 scale), and growth trajectory (does it scale with the service?). Rank by the product of all three to produce the reduction roadmap.
Examples
Automating Context Window Management
Context overflow is one of the highest-frequency toil sources for agentic services. The fix is an automated summarization step that runs before the context reaches the model's limit.

# Automated context window management
# Eliminates manual context truncation toil

from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

MODEL = "claude-sonnet-4-5"
CONTEXT_LIMIT = 180_000    # tokens; leave headroom below actual limit
SUMMARY_TARGET = 40_000    # compact to this many tokens when limit approached


def estimate_tokens(messages: list[dict]) -> int:
    """Rough estimate: 1 token per 4 characters."""
    total_chars = sum(len(str(m.get("content", ""))) for m in messages)
    return total_chars // 4


def compress_context(messages: list[dict], system: str) -> list[dict]:
    """
    When context approaches CONTEXT_LIMIT, summarize the oldest messages
    and replace them with a compact summary, keeping the most recent turns.
    This function eliminates manual context management toil.
    """
    current_tokens = estimate_tokens(messages)
    if current_tokens < CONTEXT_LIMIT:
        return messages   # no compression needed

    # Keep the last 8 turns verbatim for immediacy
    recent = messages[-8:]
    older = messages[:-8]

    if not older:
        return messages   # cannot compress further

    # Summarize the older portion
    summary_prompt = (
        "Summarize the following conversation history concisely, "
        "preserving all facts, decisions, and context needed to continue:\n\n"
        + "\n".join(f"{m['role']}: {m['content']}" for m in older)
    )
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL,
        max_tokens=2000,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": summary_prompt}]
    )
    summary_text = resp.content[0].text

    # Replace older history with summary
    compressed = [
        {"role": "user",      "content": f"[Context summary from earlier]:\n{summary_text}"},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "Understood. I have the earlier context."},
    ] + recent

    print(f"Context compressed: {current_tokens} -> ~{estimate_tokens(compressed)} est. tokens")
    return compressed


def run_agent_turn(messages: list[dict], user_input: str, system: str) -> tuple[str, list[dict]]:
    messages = compress_context(messages, system)   # auto-manage context
    messages.append({"role": "user", "content": user_input})

    resp = client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL,
        max_tokens=4096,
        system=system,
        messages=messages
    )
    reply = resp.content[0].text
    messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": reply})
    return reply, messages
Agent-Mediated Runbook Execution

Runbooks that have been executed identically five or more times are candidates for agent-mediated execution. The agent reads the runbook, uses tools to execute each step, and escalates to a human only when a step requires judgment that the runbook does not encode.
Real-World Example

A team running a document-processing agent service discovers that 52% of engineering time is consumed by toil during their first toil survey. The breakdown: 38% prompt tweaking (model behavior changes after each minor version update), 24% manual context window monitoring (checking log files and truncating histories by hand), 20% model rollout validation (running a 47-step manual checklist), and 18% ticket triage for agent failures.

Using the prioritization scoring framework, they rank context window management (high hours, easy to automate, scales linearly) and rollout validation (moderate hours, easy to automate, high growth risk) as the top two targets. In sprint 1, they implement the automated context summarization function: context overflow incidents drop to zero and 29 hours per month are freed. In sprint 2, they write a CI pipeline that runs the 47-step rollout checklist automatically: the 14-hour manual process becomes a 20-minute automated job. In sprint 3, they build an LLM-based ticket classifier that routes agent failure tickets to the correct team: ticket triage drops from 18 hours to 4 hours per month.

After three sprints of toil reduction work, total toil has dropped from 52% to 28% of engineering capacity, below the Google SRE 50% threshold. The freed capacity funds a reliability improvement project that reduces incident count by 40% over the next quarter.
Common Mistakes
Automating toil before understanding it:
jumping to automation before the inventory and prioritization steps wastes engineering effort on low-value targets and often produces brittle automations.
Not measuring toil in hours:
abstract categories like 'prompt tweaking feels like a lot' cannot be prioritized or tracked over time. The survey must produce hours-per-month figures.
Treating novel LLM failures as toil:
investigating a new model failure mode requires engineering judgment and is not toil. Only known, repeatable responses to known failure patterns qualify.
Ignoring automation maintenance toil:
an automated process that breaks monthly and requires two hours to fix is itself generating toil. Track automation maintenance cost as a separate toil category.
No human escalation path for agent-automated runbooks:
a runbook agent will encounter situations the runbook does not cover. Without an escalation tool, the agent will either fail silently or take incorrect actions.
One-time survey, no recurring measurement:
toil grows with the service. A survey done once and never repeated cannot detect new toil sources that emerge as the system scales.
Best Practices
Measure before you build:
complete the toil inventory and score every candidate before writing a single line of automation code. Prioritization data prevents investing in the wrong target.
Set a toil budget:
cap total toil at 33% of engineering capacity as an early warning threshold before reaching the 50% danger zone. If a quarter ends above 33%, toil reduction work takes priority in the next quarter.
Automate the measurement itself:
integrate toil categories into the ticket and alert systems so that categorization happens at the point of work, not retrospectively in a survey.
Use agents to reduce agent toil:
the agentic AI operations team is uniquely positioned to use LLM agents to automate operational workflows. Ticket triage, runbook execution, and anomaly explanation are all viable agent targets.
Require runbook automation at five executions:
establish a team norm that any runbook executed identically five or more times must have an automation ticket created. This prevents the accumulation of high-frequency manual procedures.
Track payback explicitly:
for each automation investment, record the expected payback period. Review actual payback against predicted payback at the next MRR. This improves future prioritization accuracy.
Celebrate toil elimination:
make toil reduction a visible organizational achievement. 'We automated X, freeing Y hours per month' belongs in sprint reviews and team all-hands, not buried in Jira.
Performance & Scalability
Toil Metrics Over Time

Track toil metrics monthly and plot trends. Key leading indicators are: toil hours growth rate (is toil growing faster than headcount?), automatable toil percentage (what proportion of current toil is theoretically automatable?), and time-to-automation for newly identified toil (how long before a survey-identified toil item has an automation in production?).

# Monthly toil trend reporting
# Run as part of the MRR pre-meeting data pipeline

from pathlib import Path
import json

def load_monthly_toil(month: str) -> dict | None:
    path = Path(f"reliability-reviews/{month}/toil-log.json")
    if not path.exists():
        return None
    return json.loads(path.read_text())

def toil_trend_report(months: list[str]) -> None:
    print(f"{'Month':<10} {'Total h':>8} {'%Capacity':>10} {'Automatable%':>14} {'Top Category':<28}")
    print("-" * 75)
    for month in months:
        data = load_monthly_toil(month)
        if not data:
            print(f"{month:<10} {'N/A':>8}")
            continue
        top = max(data.get("by_category", {}).items(), key=lambda x: x[1], default=("none", 0))
        print(
            f"{month:<10}"
            f"{data['total_toil_hours']:>8.1f}"
            f"{data['toil_pct_of_capacity']:>9.1f}%"
            f"{data.get('automatable_pct', 0):>13.1f}%"
            f"  {top[0]:<28}"
        )

# Example output:
# Month      Total h  %Capacity  Automatable%  Top Category
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2026-01      148.5      46.4%          65.3%  prompt_tweaking
# 2026-02      141.0      44.1%          66.1%  prompt_tweaking
# 2026-03       98.0      30.6%          55.1%  runbook_execution    <- automation shipped
# 2026-04       79.5      24.8%          48.1%  on_call_interrupt    <- further improvement
# 2026-05       68.0      21.3%          41.2%  on_call_interrupt
Tooling & Ecosystem
Time tracking:
Clockify, Harvest, or Jira work logs with custom toil categories. The key requirement is low-friction entry — if logging takes more than 30 seconds, engineers will skip it.
Alert analytics:
PagerDuty Analytics and OpsGenie Reporting both provide alert frequency, response time, and resolution time reports — the primary signal for on-call interrupt toil.
Runbook automation:
Rundeck, Ansible Tower, and custom LLM agent workflows. For agentic operations teams, building runbook execution agents on top of the existing LLM infrastructure is often the fastest path.
LLM observability for prompt toil:
Langfuse and Helicone both track prompt versions and output quality over time, making prompt drift visible before it becomes a manual remediation task.
CI for model validation:
PromptFoo, LangTest, and custom evaluation harnesses automate the model version validation checklist, eliminating the most common source of model rollout toil.
Comparison with Alternatives
Several approaches address operational burden in agentic AI teams. The table compares them against systematic toil programs.
Approach
Identifies root toil?
Quantifies cost?
Scalable?
Risk
Systematic toil program
Yes — structured survey + log
Yes — hours/month per category
Yes — recurring measurement cycle
Overhead of survey and tracking
Hire more engineers
No — scales the toil, not eliminates it
No
No — toil still grows with traffic
Expensive and does not fix root cause
Ad-hoc automation
Partially — whoever notices
No
No — misses low-visibility toil
Random prioritization, uncounted toil
Better tooling investment
Partially
No
Sometimes
May address symptoms, not root causes
Security & Reliability
Agent-executed runbooks must be scoped to minimum permissions:
a runbook agent should only have access to the services and actions listed in the runbook. Broad permissions create a large blast radius for an agent making an incorrect decision.
Require audit logs for all agent actions:
every tool call made by a runbook agent must be logged with timestamp, input, output, and the incident context that triggered it. These logs are essential for post-incident review.
Human approval gates for destructive operations:
restarts, rollbacks, and data truncations should require human confirmation even when the surrounding steps are automated. An approval step costs seconds and prevents irreversible mistakes.
Test runbook automations against synthetic incidents:
before relying on an automated runbook in production, exercise it against a staging environment incident to confirm it takes correct actions and escalates at the right points.
Prompt injection risk in ticket triage agents:

a ticket triage agent that reads user-submitted content could be manipulated by adversarial ticket content. Sanitize inputs and restrict the agent's action scope to classification only, never execution.
Mental Model
Think of toil as
organizational debt that compounds at the interest rate of service growth

. Unlike technical debt, which you might choose to carry indefinitely if the interest rate is low, toil debt always carries a positive interest rate: as the service grows, each unit of toil multiplies. A team that ignores toil at 30% capacity consumption will find itself at 60% capacity consumption without any change in the underlying system.
The six-criterion definition from Google SRE is a
scalpel, not a bludgeon

. Not all unpleasant work is toil. Investigating a novel model failure mode is not toil — it is engineering. Writing a postmortem is not toil — it is knowledge creation. Designing a new prompt strategy is not toil — it is product work. Only work that is all six: manual, repetitive, automatable, tactical, without enduring value, and linearly scaling — is true toil.

The uniquely powerful opportunity in agentic AI operations is that the team operates the tools needed to reduce their own toil. A ticket triage problem is solved by building a ticket triage agent. A runbook execution problem is solved by building a runbook execution agent. The team that operates AI agents is the team best positioned to use AI agents to automate their own operational work — a
virtuous cycle
if the prioritization is right and the safety constraints are in place.
Key Takeaways
Toil is precisely defined:
manual, repetitive, automatable, tactical, without enduring value, and linearly scaling with service growth. All six criteria must be met.
Agentic AI introduces four unique toil categories
not present in conventional SRE: prompt tweaking, context window management, model version rollouts, and repeating runbook execution.
Measure toil in hours per month before automating:
a structured survey producing hours-per-category data is the only basis for correct prioritization.
Prioritize by impact x automation feasibility x growth trajectory:
not all automatable toil is worth automating. The product of these three scores ranks candidates correctly.
Cap toil at 50% (target 33%) of engineering capacity.
Above 50%, the team cannot improve the service — it can only maintain it.
LLM agents can automate AI operations toil:
ticket triage, runbook execution, and context management are all viable targets — with appropriate permission scoping, audit logging, and human escalation paths.
Measure toil monthly and repeat the inventory cycle:
a one-time survey captures today's toil. Only recurring measurement detects new toil sources as the service evolves.
